LOOK.text: A single reclining chair facing the monitor. Clinical. Cold. The armrests are worn smooth by anxious hands. The seat cushion is still warm. How long ago did Patient Zero leave? Minutes? Hours?

USE.text: I sit down in the chair. It adjusts to my weight automatically. The monitor's glow washes over me. For a moment, I feel like I've been here before. Like I've always been here.

TALK.text: I whisper to the empty chair: 'Who sat here before me?' The leather creaks. Not an answer. Just old material settling. Or is it?

PICK.text: I check the crevices of the chair. A single hair. A crumb. And scratched into the armrest: 'You'll understand when you see the screen.'

OPEN.text: I recline the chair to its fully flat position. Staring at the ceiling. Fluorescent lights buzz. This is what therapy looks like in the metaverse.

CLOSE.text: I return the chair to upright. It faces the monitor. Everything in this room faces the monitor. By design.

PUSH.text: I push the chair. It rolls on its wheels, stopping directly in front of the monitor. As if drawn to it magnetically. Or programmatically.

PULL.text: I pull the chair away from the monitor. It feels wrong. Like the room itself resists. The chair belongs in front of that screen.

GIVE.text: The chair has received enough patients. Their weight. Their fears. Their wallet histories. It doesn't need more.

LOOK.text: The monitor shows wallet transaction history. Wait... those are MY transactions. The mints, the swaps, the failed transactions. Every gas fee, every approval. The charts on the wall... they're MY portfolio. Patient Zero... is ME?

then.text: The monitor still shows my complete history. Every trade. Every mistake. Every moment of hope. It's all here. It was always here.

else.text: The monitor hums to life. Green text on black screen...
It shows... a wallet address. Wait.
Those are MY transactions.
The charts on the wall... they're MY portfolio. Every trade. Every mint. Every mistake.
Patient Zero... is ME?
"Congratulations. You found yourself."
"The first step to recovery is admitting you have a problem."
"The second step? ...There is no second step in crypto."
PATIENT ZERO — Identity Revealed

TALK.text: I talk to the monitor. It displays: 'VOICE RECOGNIZED. WELCOME BACK.' Back? I've never been here. ...have I?

PICK.text: The monitor is mounted to the wall with industrial bolts. It's not going anywhere. Neither is the truth it displays.

OPEN.text: I try to access the system files. The monitor shows: 'ADMIN ACCESS REQUIRED. Nice try, Patient Zero.'

CLOSE.text: I try to turn off the monitor. It flickers but stays on. It's been on this whole time. Waiting for me to arrive. It won't stop now.

PUSH.text: I push the monitor. It doesn't budge. It's part of the wall. Part of the room. Part of the answer.

PULL.text: I pull at the monitor's edge. Behind it, wires run into the wall and deep into the building. This room is the heart of the clinic.

GIVE.text: The monitor has already been given everything. My data. My history. My identity. It doesn't need anything else from me.

LOOK.text: Charts everywhere. Pinned, taped, nailed to every inch of wall. Every candle, every rug, every pump and dump. A complete history of someone's crypto journey. Annotations in frantic handwriting: 'THIS IS THE BOTTOM' written seven times at different price points. Someone who looks a lot like... me.

USE.text: I trace the charts with my finger. The timeline starts optimistic. 'FIRST NFT MINT!' Then it gets darker. 'WHY DID I APPROVE THAT CONTRACT.' The last entry just says: 'Again.'

TALK.text: I read the annotations aloud. 'Buy here.' 'Sell here.' 'Why didn't I sell here.' 'LIQUIDATED.' It's like reading my own diary. Because it is.

PICK.text: I pull one chart off the wall. Behind it, another chart. Behind that, another. It's charts all the way down. Like layers of regret.

OPEN.text: I unfold a rolled-up chart in the corner. A complete map of DeFi protocols I've interacted with. Red Xs on the ones that rugged. It's mostly red Xs.

CLOSE.text: I pin the chart back. The wall stares at me with a thousand data points. All mine.

PUSH.text: I push a stack of charts aside. They cascade to the floor like falling dominoes. Like falling prices. Like falling hopes.

PULL.text: I pull a chart free from the wall. A sticky note falls: 'This is not financial advice. This is a cry for help.'

GIVE.text: The walls have enough charts. Enough data. Enough pain. They don't need more.

LOOK.text: The door back to the reception. Simple. Unassuming. Do I leave? Or do I stay and face the music? The monitor hums behind me. The charts watch from the walls. The chair waits.

then.text: Time to face the world. With the truth.

else.text: Not yet. There's still something I need to see in this room.

TALK.text: I whisper to the door: 'I'll be back.' The door says nothing. Doors are good at that.

PICK.text: I can't take the door. But I can take what I've learned. Maybe. Probably not. I'll definitely make the same mistakes.

then.text: Time to face the world. With the truth.

else.text: Not yet. There's still something I need to see in this room.

CLOSE.text: The door is already closed. Keeping the treatment room's secrets contained. For now.

then.text: Time to face the world. With the truth.

else.text: Not yet. There's still something I need to see in this room.

PULL.text: I pull the door toward me. A draft of cold air from the reception. Reality waiting on the other side.

GIVE.text: The door doesn't need anything. It just needs me to decide: stay or go.

then.text: A handwritten note on the table. Adrian's handwriting.

else.text: A small table with papers. I should look at the monitor first.

then.text: I've already read it. Words that changed everything.

else.text: The note reads:
"If you're reading this, the game worked."
"You're not a patient. You're a builder."
"The patients never make it this far — they stay in the lobby refreshing charts."
"You explored. You solved puzzles. You made it to the end."
"That makes you different. Welcome to the team."
"— Adrian"
I should check the monitor first.

USE.text: It's a note. I should try to pick it up or look at it more closely.

TALK.text: It's a piece of paper. It's not going to talk back.

CLOSE.text: I could fold it, but why? The words are what matter.

LOOK.text: Dark, sterile flooring. Scuff marks everywhere, like someone paced back and forth for hours. Or days. The marks form a pattern... is that a candlestick chart worn into the floor?

USE.text: I kneel and touch the floor. Cold. Clinical. Like the moment you realize your 'diamond hands' are actually just frozen from fear.

TALK.text: I whisper to the floor: 'How many patients have you held up?' The silence is deafening. The floor has seen things. Terrible, terrible portfolios.

PICK.text: A tiny screw on the floor. From the chair, maybe. Everything in this room is falling apart.

OPEN.text: There's no trapdoor. This isn't that kind of adventure game. Though a secret exit would explain how Patient Zero escaped.

CLOSE.text: You can't close a floor. Trust me, I've tried closing positions that were just as impossible.

PUSH.text: I push against the floor with my foot. Solid. Unlike the floor price of most NFT collections I've invested in.

PULL.text: I try to pull up a floor tile. Nope. This floor is more firmly committed than any liquidity provider I've ever met.

GIVE.text: The floor already has my footprints. And my tears. And probably some spilled coffee. It doesn't need more.

LOOK.text: A golden plaque reads: 'To the Founders who believed before anyone else. The clinic remembers.' Below it, your wallet address is engraved. How long has this been here?

USE.text: I touch the plaque. It's warm. The engraving shifts — showing my $ZERO balance, my first transaction, my time in the ecosystem. Patient Zero was always a founder.

TALK.text: I whisper to the plaque: 'Was it worth it?' The golden letters rearrange: 'You're still here, aren't you?'

LOOK.text: Exposed pipes and cables snake across the ceiling like a messy dependency graph. Some are wrapped in tape. The infrastructure here is held together by hope and duct tape. Just like Layer 2 scaling solutions.

USE.text: I reach for a dangling cable. Probably not a good idea. Last time I touched something I didn't understand, I approved an unlimited token allowance.

TALK.text: I address the tangle of wires: 'You look like my transaction history on Etherscan.' The cables say nothing. They're better organized than my DeFi positions.

PICK.text: I tug on a loose cable. The lights flicker. Maybe I should leave the infrastructure alone. I'm a detective, not a plumber.

OPEN.text: I pry open a junction box. Inside: wires labeled 'DATA IN', 'DATA OUT', and one labeled 'PATIENT ZERO MONITORING'. Ominous.

CLOSE.text: I snap the junction box shut. Some things are better left sealed. Like that Uniswap LP position from 2021.

PUSH.text: I push a pipe. It groans. The whole ceiling shudders. Infrastructure this old should be migrated to a new protocol.

PULL.text: I pull a cable gently. Somewhere in the building, something beeps. I let go immediately. Not today, unexpected consequences.

GIVE.text: The pipes need a plumber, not a crypto detective with a hero complex.

LOOK.text: A cold metal side table. Medical grade. A half-empty glass of water sits on it, still sweating. Whoever was here last didn't finish their drink. They left in a hurry. Or got liquidated mid-sip.

USE.text: I take a sip of the water. Stale. Tastes like regret and fluoride. Two things I'm very familiar with.

TALK.text: 'Table, tell me your secrets.' It remains silent. Tables are the strong, silent type. Like validators during network congestion.

PICK.text: A glass of water with fingerprints on it. Still half full. Or half empty, depending on your portfolio.

OPEN.text: The table has a small drawer. Inside: a pen, a paperclip, and a sticky note that says 'REMEMBER: YOU ARE THE PATIENT.' Helpful.

CLOSE.text: I close the tiny drawer. Click. The sound of finality. Like closing a leveraged position at a loss.

PUSH.text: I push the table. It scrapes across the floor with a screech that could wake dead protocols.

PULL.text: I pull the table toward the chair. Now it's within arm's reach. Convenience. The one thing DeFi has never offered.

GIVE.text: The table holds things. That's its entire purpose. It's the cold storage of furniture.

LOOK.text: A massive observation window, completely shattered. Through the spider-web of cracks I can see another room — a recliner, filing cabinets, monitors. Someone was watching from in there. Watching the patients. Watching ME. The glass broke from the inside. Whoever was observing... tried to get out.

USE.text: I peer through a gap in the shattered glass. The observation room is dark now. Dusty. A coffee mug sits on the desk, still half full. Whoever was watching Patient Zero left in a hurry. Or maybe they realized they were watching themselves.

TALK.text: 'Hello? Anyone still in there?' My voice echoes through the broken glass and dies in the empty room. The only answer is the hum of a monitor left on standby. Even the observers got liquidated eventually.

PICK.text: I carefully pull a shard of glass from the frame. It catches the light from the monitor — green text reflected in the fragment. For a second, I see my own face in it. Patient. Observer. Same person.

OPEN.text: There's no door to the observation room from this side. One-way access. Like a smart contract with no withdraw function. The observers could watch, but they were never meant to intervene.

CLOSE.text: Can't close a broken window. Can't undo a broken transaction. Some things just stay shattered and you learn to navigate the edges.

PUSH.text: I push against the remaining glass. A section gives way and crashes into the observation room. The sound is obscene in this silence. Like a margin call at 3 AM.

PULL.text: I pull at the window frame. It groans but holds. The frame is stronger than the glass. The system survives even when transparency is destroyed. Sound familiar?

GIVE.text: The window has already been given enough — someone's fist, apparently. Or maybe their forehead. Depends on how bad the portfolio charts looked that day.

LOOK.text: Thick cables and tubes snake out from under the chair and disappear into the floor through crude holes in the tile. Some carry data. Some carry... fluids? The labels are worn off. Whatever the chair was connected to, it ran deep. Deeper than the basement. Deeper than any blockchain explorer could index.

USE.text: I follow a cable with my hand. It's warm. Pulsing slightly. Still active. Whatever system this chair feeds into, it hasn't been shut down. Patient Zero's session was never terminated. It's been running in the background this whole time.

TALK.text: 'Where do you go?' I ask the cables. They hum in response. A low frequency, like a node syncing blocks. They know where they go. They just don't answer to patients.

PICK.text: I try to pull a cable loose. It won't budge. Bolted to the floor. These aren't decorative — they're load-bearing infrastructure. Like the one validator keeping a chain alive.

OPEN.text: I pry open a floor panel where the cables converge. Beneath: a junction box labeled 'BIOMETRIC FEED — TREATMENT SUITE A.' This chair wasn't just reading wallets. It was reading bodies.

CLOSE.text: I push the floor panel back. Some endpoints shouldn't be exposed. I learned that the hard way with an unsecured API. And with this floor.

PUSH.text: I step on the cables. They resist, thick and industrial. Whoever installed these expected them to last. Built to outlast the patients. Built to outlast the clinic itself.

PULL.text: I yank a thinner cable free. Sparks. The monitor flickers for a second. I plug it back in immediately. Not the time to perform surgery on a system I don't understand. Again.

GIVE.text: The cables are already carrying everything they need. Data, power, maybe secrets. They're the most honest things in this room. They just... transmit.

LOOK.text: The wall near the door is in rough shape. Paint peeling in sheets, water damage creeping down from the ceiling, dark stains that could be rust or... I'm going to say rust. This clinic has been deteriorating for a long time. Maintained just enough to function. Never enough to look healthy. Like most DeFi protocols in a bear market.

USE.text: I scrape at the peeling paint with my fingernail. Layer after layer. The wall has been repainted a dozen times. Each layer a different shade of clinical white. Each renovation a fresh coat over the same rot. Remind you of any token rebrands?

TALK.text: 'How many treatments have you witnessed?' I ask the wall. It answers by dropping a flake of paint onto my shoe. Passive-aggressive. Walls in crypto clinics have attitude.

PICK.text: I peel off a strip of paint. Behind it, someone scratched into the plaster: 'NGMI.' Below that, in different handwriting: 'WAGMI.' Below that: 'We didn't.'

OPEN.text: There's a hairline crack in the wall. I widen it with my fingers. Behind the plaster: old wiring and a faded blueprint fragment. The room was originally labeled 'RECONDITIONING BAY 01.' Reconditioning. Not treatment.

CLOSE.text: I press the paint flap back against the wall. It holds for a second, then curls away again. Some things refuse to stay hidden once exposed. Like a rug pull's paper trail.

PUSH.text: I push against the wall. It's solid beneath the decay. The bones of this building are strong. It's just the surface that's falling apart. Story of every crypto project with good fundamentals and terrible marketing.

PULL.text: I pull a loose chunk of plaster free. Dust rains down. Behind it, the raw concrete of the original structure. Before the paint. Before the clinic. Before Patient Zero. This building has always been here, waiting.

GIVE.text: The wall has been given enough abuse by time, moisture, and whoever punched that dent near the baseboard. It doesn't need my contribution.

else.text: PATIENT ZERO — Identity Revealed

lines: You were never looking for someone else.
Unlike most exchanges during a bank run.

else.text: GAME COMPLETE: ZEROadventure II

then.text: The monitor still shows my floppy data alongside my wallet history. Two collections. One story.

else.text: I hold the Floppy Disc Box up to the monitor. My hands are shaking.
The display splits. On the left: my wallet history. On the right: the floppy collection's on-chain data. The timestamps match.
Every floppy I minted corresponds to a moment in my crypto journey. Each one a snapshot. A backup of who I was at every turning point.
'Patient Zero's treatment was never about curing crypto addiction. It was about preserving the story. YOUR story. And you kept the floppies. You kept the proof.'
The screen fades to a single word in golden text: 'PRESERVED.'
PRESERVED — Your floppies hold the story of Patient Zero

lines: The treatment room. One chair. One monitor.
Dr. Satoshi said not everyone is ready for what's inside.
...has been in your wallet all along.
